item.title: America’s allies look to declare independence from the US
item.description: Traditional partners are rethinking their economic ties

item.title: Waiting for Andy: what will a Burnham premiership mean?
item.description: It is the threat of Reform UK that has so panicked Labour MPs

item.title: The cooler corner of Côtes du Rhône
item.description: In an under-sung swathe of the prolific appellation, small-scale, independent producers are crafting fresh, quality wines — and raising expectations

item.title: Burnham cemented himself as Labour’s winner
item.description: A coronation for the PM hopeful looks likely — unless Keir Starmer decides to fight back

item.title: Leverage in the financial system, revisited
item.description: Plus, the dollar comeback

item.title: Burnham will have to be brave if he wants to get Britain growing again
item.description: Unfortunately UK politics is hostile to making tough, but necessary, choices

item.title: Time to switch off your landing light at night?
item.description: The Butterfly Conservation is urging protection of moth populations . . . but the lure of the lamp remains strong

item.title: What would a stablecoin run look like?
item.description: Fire sales of assets held in reserve could trigger problems in many other financial markets

item.title: Why sinodollars outweigh the petroyuan
item.description: China’s currency is becoming a more important part of the global financial system but the dollar is still in control

item.title: Back-stabbing myths are driving the US and Europe further apart
item.description: Competing narratives of abandonment — from Iran to Greenland — could lead to Nato’s permanent unravelling

item.title: We need to talk about Brexit
item.description: Britain must rebuild EU ties, but trying to rejoin would for now be a diversion

item.title: Reform UK has a new set of problems
item.description: The fight for the right is far from settled, as the Makerfield and Aberdeen by-elections showed

item.title: Rejoining the EU is no panacea
item.description: Reversing Brexit would sidetrack UK parliament from pursuing more impactful growth reforms

item.title: Sorry, Elon, the far right is still the far right
item.description: And on some issues, it’s further to the right than before

item.title: Workers are emerging as the next big AI logjam
item.description: Big Tech wakes up to need for brawn and crafts skills to build and maintain data centres

item.title: Finally, a use for corporate bullshit
item.description: Companies might one day be able to use it to choose who to hire and promote

item.title: The new chair effect
item.description: First impressions, priced in

item.title: Markets get the measure of Trump
item.description: At the very first sign that the president is buckling on an unorthodox foreign policy decision, that is the time to pounce

item.title: Europe’s stocks offer a peace dividend their US peers can’t match
item.description: European companies have more to gain by way of recovery if energy shortages caused by the Iran conflict ease

item.title: Rathbones’ blunder boosts banks in the race for the UK’s rich
item.description: An internal review that found regulatory compliance shortcomings sent the stock down 17%

item.title: Harry Potter has ruined Britain
item.description: Our greatest assets have been appropriated by the Ministry of Wizardry

item.title: ChatGPT moved my cheese: AI is unsettling the self-help shelf
item.description: Instant summaries sound the death knell for the bullet-point books that prey on our insecurities

item.title: Why dignity is the measure that matters
item.description: From litter-pickers to cage fighters, the signs of a society’s health are often hidden in plain sight

item.title: Take our phones too! Let’s add adults to the social media ban
item.description: We have to face up to the fact that we’re all addicted to online scrolling

item.title: The window for peace in Ukraine won’t be open forever
item.description: There is an opportunity to freeze the conflict, but Putin’s fantasy of total victory could get in the way
